--- SOURCE 3: Towards eliminating the use of animals for regulatory ... ---
URL: https://www.altex.org/index.php/altex/article/view/815

SUMMARY:
by C Hendriksen · 2006 · Cited by 16 — Implementation of the consistency approach might significantly contribute towards the elimination of the use of animals in regulatory required vaccine quality

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.altex.org/plugins/themes/bootstrap3/img/3R_Logo.png)
![ALTEX](https://www.altex.org/public/journals/1/pageHeaderLogoImage_en_US.png)

# Towards eliminating the use of animals for regulatory required vaccine quality control

## Article Sidebar

![](https://www.altex.org/public/journals/1/cover_issue_48_en_US.png)

## Main Article Content

## Abstract

Traditionally, regulatory required vaccine quality control relies on the use of laboratory animals. Both batch testing for safety and testing for potency are based on anima I models. Quite often these models include procedures that induce severe pain and suffering. This has urged the development of 3Rs methods. Some examples of recent achievements in the quality control of toxoid vaccines are the replacement of challenge procedures by serological methods, the reduction of numbers of animals required by changing from multi-dose to single dose testing, and developments in the area of *in vitro* models and physicochemical techniques. Central in the progress towards the elimination of animal use is the acceptance of the so-called consistency approach. The essence of the consistency approach is that a new batch of vaccine is no longer seen as a unique product but as only one of a series of batches produced from the same starting material (seed lot). Consequently, the new batch shares many of the characteristics of the previous batches produced from the same seed lot. This allows for a new strategy of vaccine quality control consisting of demonstrating consistency in production, placing emphasis on aspects such as in-process testing, and implementing Good Manufacturing Practice and Quality Assurance (QA). The new strategy particularly focuses on non-animal test models such as physicochemical methods and *in vitro* models. Implementation of the consistency approach might significantly contribute towards the elimination of the use of animals in regulatory required vaccine quality control.

--- SOURCE 4: ANIMAL HEALTH FORUM ON VETERINARY VACCINES ... ---
URL: https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/2025/06/2025-ahf-finalreport-final.pdf

SUMMARY:
Global regulatory harmonisation is urgent to ensure safe, effective, and accessible vaccines, including recognition of autogenous vaccines in specific contexts.

FULL CONTENT:
Original: English May 2025 ANIMAL HEALTH FORUM ON VETERINARY VACCINES AND VACCINATION From science to action – reflections for change 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 2 Contents 1. Introduction .................................................................................................................................... 3 2. High Level Panels .......................................................................................................................... 3 2.1. High Level Panel 1: Vaccination decision – desired effects and undesired consequences ........................................................................................................................ 3 2.2. High Level Panel 2: Vaccine availability and access – time is of the essence...................... 4 3. Technical Item ‘Veterinary vaccines and vaccination – from science to action: reflections for change’ .................................................................................................................. 4 3.1. Session 1: To vaccinate or not to vaccinate. That is the question. ....................................... 4 3.2. Session 2: Bridging gaps to enhance vaccine availability ..................................................... 6 3.3. Session 3: Overcoming barriers to ensure timely access to quality vaccines ....................... 7 3.4. Session 4: Strategic Vaccination: Paving the Path to a Healthier Future ............................. 8 3.5. Session 5: Trade and societal acceptance: the ‘x’ and ‘y’ on the vaccination equation ................................................................................................................................. 9 3.6. Session 6: Final discussion, wrap up and conclusions ....................................................... 11 4. Conclusion.................................................................................................................................... 11 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 3 1. Introduction Vaccination is one of the most effective tools available to Veterinary Services for the prevention and control of animal diseases. Its proper use supports animal health and welfare, reduces reliance on antimicrobials, enhances food security, sustains livelihoods, and contributes to the protection of public health and biodiversity. However, the full potential of vaccines remains underutilised due to persistent challenges in vaccine development, registration, distribution, and uptake. The 2025 Animal Health Forum, held during the 92nd WOAH General Session, was designed to bridge the gap between technical recommendations and field-level implementation. The Forum followed the presentation of the Technical Item titled “Veterinary vaccines and vaccination – from science to action: reflections for change” authored by Dr Baptiste Dungu and Dr Meritxell Donadeu (Doc. 92GS/Tech-01), which outlined key scientific, regulatory, and practical challenges affecting animal vaccine use globally. Structured across six technical sessions, the Forum provided a platform for WOAH Delegates, experts, and partners to engage in solution-focused discussions addressing three interconnected domains: vaccine availability, vaccine access, and vaccine demand (all of which influence vaccine adoption). Special attention was given to trade implications, societal acceptance, and the enabling environment required to build resilient, sustainable, and science-based vaccination systems. All sessions were recorded and are available for public viewing online, allowing a wider audience to benefit from the insights and exchanges shared during the event. The online recordings can be accessed here. The Forum outcomes—together with the Technical Item and the shared experiences of Members— informed Resolution 29, which was adopted by the General Assembly, and which will shape the future direction of WOAH’s work on veterinary vaccination. 2. High Level Panels 2.1. High Level Panel 1: Vaccination decision – desired effects and undesired consequences 2.1.1. Moderator Dr Gregorio Torres, Head of Science Department, World Organisation for Animal Health 2.1.2. Panellists: Minister Luis Planas Puchades (Spain; Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food); Dr Huyam Salih (African Union – Inter-African Bureau for Animal Resources); Minister H.E. CS Sen. Mutahi Kagwe (Kenya; Cabinet Secretary for Agriculture and Livestock Development); Minister Sidi Tiémoko Touré (Côte d’Ivoire; Ministre des Ressources Animales et Halieutiques de la République de Côte d’Ivoire). 2.1.3. Overview This panel explored high-level experiences and reflections on when and how vaccination should be deployed as part of national disease control strategies. Speakers shared recent examples from Spain, Kenya, Côte d’Ivoire and the African Union, underlining the importance of aligning vaccination decisions with public health, economic priorities, and trade objectives. Communication emerged as a critical success factor, with calls to better engage communities, counter misinformation, and integrate social research into vaccination campaign planning. Panellists agreed that WOAH plays a vital role in supporting coordination, standard-setting, and global, regional, and subregional cooperation in the face of cross-border health threats. 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 4 2.2. High Level Panel 2: Vaccine availability and access – time is of the essence 2.2.1. Moderator Dr Javier Yugueros-Marcos, Head of the Antimicrobial Resistance and Veterinary Products Department, World Organisation for Animal Health 2.2.2. Panellists: Dr Marc Prikazsky (Ceva Santé Animale; President and CEO, representing Health for Animals); Dr Derrick Sim (Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; Managing Director of Vaccine Markets, Health Security and COVAX); Minister El Moctar Gaguih Ebeyite (Mauritania; Ministre de l’Elevage); Ms Marianne Grosclaude (World Bank; Manager for Global Engagement of Department for Agriculture and Food). 2.2.3. Overview This session focused on the urgent need to improve vaccine availability and access through strong public-private collaboration. Dr Prikazsky emphasised the importance of risk-sharing models, innovation, and visibility of demand to incentivise vaccine development and mitigate supply chain delays. Dr Sim shared lessons from human health, highlighting the role of long-term investment, partner alignment, and readiness systems to ensure timely and equitable access. Minister Gaguih Ebeyite and Ms Marianne underlined the importance of strong veterinary systems, reliable infrastructure, and financial mechanisms to reach underserved populations. Panellists agreed on the critical role WOAH plays in promoting strategic partnerships, sustainable financing, and international collaboration as essential elements to overcoming persistent access barriers. 3. Technical Item ‘Veterinary vaccines and vaccination – from science to action: reflections for change’ The Technical Item titled “Veterinary vaccines and vaccination – from science to action: reflections for change” (Doc. 92GS/Tech-01) was presented by the Rapporteurs, Dr Baptiste Dungu (Initiative for Animal Diseases, and member of the WOAH Scientific Commission for Animal Diseases) and Dr Meritxell Donadeu (Initiative for Neglected Animal Diseases, consultant for the World Health Organization, and honorary Senior Fellow of the University of Melbourne, Australia). The presentation provided an overview of the current landscape of veterinary vaccines, highlighting global and regional gaps in vaccine development, registration, delivery, and use. It explored how vaccination can be more effectively integrated into national disease control strategies, ensuring they remain science-based, economically viable, socially acceptable, and environmentally sustainable, while also avoiding unnecessary trade barriers. The Technical Item laid the foundation for Forum discussions and informed the recommendations to be considered by the Assembly. The Technical Item is available here. 3.1. Session 1: To vaccinate or not to vaccinate. That is the question. 3.1.1. Background Delegates often face tensions between societal expectations, producers’ needs, and trade considerations when deciding whether to vaccinate. This session explored how Delegates and livestock producers navigate these challenges, and how WOAH can support science-based decision-making in complex contexts. 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 5 3.1.2. Objectives • Explore how Delegates and livestock producers manage conflicts surrounding vaccination decisions. • Discuss the role of WOAH in supporting effective and science-based decision-making. 3.1.3. Moderator Dr Baptiste Dungu, Technical Item Rapporteur 3.1.4. Panellists Dr Hendrik Jan Roest (Netherlands; WOAH Alternate Delegate); Mr Julian Madeley (World Egg Organisation; Director General); Dr Allan Azegele (Kenya; WOAH Delegate); Dr Baoxu Huang (People’s Republic of China; WOAH Delegate). 3.1.5. Key messages • Vaccination against certain WOAH-listed diseases (e.g. FMD) creates trade challenges, which discourages the application of vaccines in disease control. Risk managers may accept zero risk when assessing the risks of products from vaccinated animals, while accepting some risk for products of unvaccinated animals. This inconsistent risk tolerance should be addressed. • Advances in disease knowledge, diagnostics, and surveillance over the past two decades call for a reassessment of WOAH Codes and Manuals. Current trade rules on vaccinated animals may be overly restrictive. • Share responsibility for animal health between farmers, government, and other stakeholders strengthens disease prevention, control, and surveillance. • Egg producers strongly support access to effective and well-implemented HPAI vaccination, tailored to national contexts and supported by surveillance – complementing biosecurity efforts. • The World Egg Organisation Avian Influenza Global Expert Group recognise that biosecurity alone (while still essential) is insufficient to prevent and control HPAI due to endemic virus circulation in wild birds, and that access to effective vaccination in laying hens is necessary. • To enable HPAI vaccination globally, it must be both scientifically robust and economically viable. Surveillance systems must be practical and affordable. Open communication between trading partners is essential to prevent trade barriers for countries adopting vaccination. • Trust and transparency, especially around vaccine sourcing, are essential for successful vaccination strategies. • Veterinarians, farmers and community leaders need to be engaged in designing and delivering culturally appropriate and widely accepted vaccination campaigns. • Clear and consistent communication from trusted sources is essential to counter misinformation and build public confidence in vaccination efforts. Comprehensive public awareness campaigns should be implemented to educate farmers about the safety and benefits of vaccination, addressing specific concerns. • People’s Republic of China implements compulsory vaccination for major animal diseases, backed by strong government support for R&D, financial subsidies, and extensive surveillance. These efforts help safeguard animal production and public health. • People’s Republic of China applies strict safety and efficacy evaluations, regulatory oversight, and systematic inspections of veterinary vaccines. Its comprehensive approach combines 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 6 compulsory vaccination with scientific decision-making, quality control, and tailored policy implementation to maintain trade and production confidence. 3.2. Session 2: Bridging gaps to enhance vaccine availability 3.2.1. Background Vaccine development and production are often slow and costly. Limited research funding, competing manufacturer priorities, intellectual property barriers, and lack of protocols or production standards impact the quality and suitability of veterinary vaccines. This session explored key barriers and shared experiences to help address them. 3.2.2. Objectives • Highlight key factors limiting vaccine availability, identify related challenges, and present actionable recommendations for WOAH and its Members. 3.2.3. Moderator Professor Ann Cullinane, Vice Chair of the WOAH Biological Standards Commission 3.2.4. Panellists Dr Bernadette Abela (World Health Organization; Team Leader, Neglected Zoonotic Diseases); Dr Carlos Orellana Vaquero (Chile; WOAH Delegate and Vice President of the Commission of the Americas); Dr Donald King (The Pirbright Institute, WOAH Reference Laboratory for FMD); Carolin Schumacher (GALVMed; CEO) 3.2.5. Key messages • World Health Organization Target Product Profiles must meet clear public health needs and be developed through inclusive, consensus-driven processes involving scientists, regulators, and end-users. • Vaccines and other tools must be treated as public goods, with active efforts to encourage uptake. • The Nagoya Protocol should be implemented without compromising global disease surveillance or delaying access to biological materials needed for vaccine and diagnostic development. • Vaccination is vital for sustainable aquaculture. Chile’s experience shows that vaccines reduce outbreaks and antimicrobial use, supporting productivity and food security. The FAO’s Blue Transformation recognises and promotes vaccine use as a key strategy for sustainable and equitable aquaculture intensification. • Global regulatory harmonisation is urgent to ensure safe, effective, and accessible vaccines, including recognition of autogenous vaccines in specific contexts. • Continuous vaccine development and stronger regulatory frameworks key to meeting environmental and health challenges in aquaculture. • Efficient and effective Product Development Partnerships that facilitate north-south technology transfer are essential to deliver vaccines tailored to LMIC needs. • Pay-for-result mechanisms and market incentives are more effective than pure academic research in translating science into usable products. • The veterinary sector lacks a single coordinating body like GAVI. Current efforts are fragmented and poorly aligned with farmers’ needs. Financial constraints and weak service 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 7 delivery infrastructure are major barriers, and it is unclear whether a GAVI-like set up could function in the veterinary domain. 3.3. Session 3: Overcoming barriers to ensure timely access to quality vaccines 3.3.1. Background Fragmented vaccine registration processes and national and regional regulatory inconsistencies hinder timely access to vaccines. Vaccines are often licensed based on demand, and delays in registration or procurement limit availability. The absence of harmonised registration systems discourages investment and fosters reliance on substandard products or antimicrobials. This session reviewed challenges and mechanisms, such as vaccine banks and prequalification systems, to improve vaccine access. 3.3.2. Objectives • Explore key barriers to vaccine access • Discuss mechanisms that can facilitate national procurement and access to high quality vaccines 3.3.3. Moderator Dr Baptiste Dungu, Technical Item Rapporteur; 3.3.4. Panellists Dr Teko Agbo Assiongbon (Union Economique et Monétaire Ouest Africaine); Dr Dónal Sammin (European Commission for the Control of Foot and Mouth Disease; Executive Secretary); Dr Alasdair King (MSD Animal Health; Director of International Veterinary Health Department); Ms Alka Upadhyaya (India; WOAH Delegate); Dr Sanne Charles Bodjo (The Pan African Veterinary Vaccine Centre of the African Union; Acting Director). 3.3.5. Key messages • The management of veterinary medicines, particularly vaccines, in sub-Saharan Africa is undermined by several challenges: unsupervised by farmers, inadequate storage conditions, the presence of illicit or informal distribution channels, and the circulation of counterfeit products – all of which are compounded by rising antimicrobial resistance. • Harmonised marketing authorisation procedures are needed to ensure access to safe, effective vaccines during emergencies. • Despite the widespread use of poor-quality veterinary vaccines, there is no internationally recognised system for independently assuring that veterinary vaccines comply with minimum international standards. A prequalification system for veterinary vaccines could help address this. • EuFMD has demonstrated the feasibility of prequalifying FMD vaccines to WOAH standards. Expanding the prequalification scheme could ensure cost-effective access to WOAH compliant FMD vaccines while delivering socio-economic benefits. • India’s Livestock Health and Disease Control Programme integrates domestically developed vaccines with digital tools for real-time coverage monitoring. The approach is supported by a strong regulatory framework within the constitution of an Empowered Committee on Animal Health (ECAH) and fast tracking of vaccine testing and regulatory clearances to allow emergency use. 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 8 • India promotes regional vaccine platforms through public-private partnerships and One Health-based strategies, linking R&D investment, demand forecasting, surveillance, and community engagement to strengthen sustainable vaccine delivery. • Ensuring the use of good quality vaccines is important – use of poor-quality vaccines undermines confidence in all vaccines and reduces vaccination rates. • Access also includes the means for administration. Unused vaccines are wasteful and reduce availability for others. • Planning and communication are essential. Manufacturers require long lead times to meet demand. • Regional and continental-level certification of veterinary vaccines is essential for quality assured vaccines that meet the standards for controlling animal diseases. • Harmonised audit and certification guidelines for vaccine production facilities, recognised at country, regional and international levels, can support the establishment of a trusted network of producers for emergency response. • Guidelines for emergency registration or temporary approval are essential to ensure access to quality-assured vaccines, including for neglected zoonotic diseases. 3.4. Session 4: Strategic Vaccination: Paving the Path to a Healthier Future 3.4.1. Background This session focused on technical elements for effective vaccination, including the use of epidemiological data for planning, vaccine selection, post-vaccination monitoring, and adapting surveillance to demonstrate disease freedom or support early detection. 3.4.2. Objectives • Share experiences and highlight key elements for designing cost-effective, sustainable vaccination strategies 3.4.3. Moderator Dr Meritxell Donadeu, Technical Item Rapporteur; 3.4.4. Panellists Dr Masatsugu Okita (Japan; WOAH Delegate); Dr Marie-Christine Le Gal (France; WOAH Delegate); Dr Ramanan Laxminarayan (One Health Trust; Founder and President); Dr Phil Hadley (International Meat Secretariat; Secretary General) 3.4.5. Key messages • Vaccination decisions must be tailored to each disease. A one-size-fits-all approach is not appropriate, and vaccination strategies must consider global strategies and goals (e.g. management versus elimination) and be guided by local contexts. • Robust surveillance and post-vaccination monitoring are central to early detection and demonstrating disease freedom. Guidelines exist for technical requirements, registration and pharmacovigilance of veterinary medicinal products and WOAH Members can use these to inform their vaccination programmes. • Effective vaccination requires strong biosecurity, clearly defined rules during outbreaks, and a One Health approach linking animal and public health. • Strong political and financial support, with clear, predictable implementation rules, is critical. 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 9 • Effective communication across all stakeholder levels, local to international, is essential. • Animal vaccines offer broad benefits in terms of improving animal health, farmer livelihoods and wellbeing, reducing pandemic risk, and in many instances, reducing antimicrobial use for growth promotion and disease prevention and treatment. • The benefits of reducing antimicrobial use are immediate and include lower cost of antimicrobials and improved farm productivity (lower animal mortality). • Countries should adopt measures that enable differential pricing of meat raised without antimicrobials that incentivises greater use of vaccines, ensure sustainable access to vaccines at affordable prices, and educate farmers on making the switch out of antimicrobial-based livestock rearing to a vaccine-oriented approach to herd health. • Vaccination is an important tool to protect animal health, prevent or limit disease incursion and spread, and reduce both physical and economic losses. The production sector must have access to effective, reliable, and affordable vaccines to support their appropriate and justified use. • Reducing losses through vaccination supports both animal health and sustainable production. Neither industry nor civil society can accept avoidable productivity losses when the world is challenged to meet rising protein demand while reducing environmental impact. • Industry needs an aligned position on vaccination and trade. Trade restrictions linked to vaccination discourage its adoption and undermine disease control efforts. • To increase uptake, clearer communication of vaccine benefits is needed, alongside government-led campaigns to raise awareness and provide support where appropriate. 3.5. Session 5: Trade and societal acceptance: the ‘x’ and ‘y’ on the vaccination equation 3.5.1. Background This session explored the desired and undesired consequences of vaccination, with a particular focus on trade impacts and societal acceptance. Experts discussed how vaccination decisions can influence international trade, sometimes leading to unjustified restrictions, and examined the challenges of gaining public trust and support for vaccination as a sustainable alternative to extreme disease control measures like stamping out. 3.5.2. Objectives • Discuss the impact of vaccination decisions on international trade • Examine societal perceptions and the challenges in building broad public support for vaccination-based disease control programmes 3.5.3. Moderator Dr Mary Van Andel, Chief Veterinary Officer and WOAH Delegate for New Zealand, WOAH Council Member 3.5.4. Panellists Dr Gaston Funes (World Organisation for Animal Health Terrestrial Animal Health Code Commission); Mr Rolando Alcalá (World Trade Organisation; Counsellor in Agriculture and Commodities Division); Mr Felix Metzger (International Veterinary Student Association; President); Dr Emmanuelle Soubeyran (World Organisation for Animal Health; Director General). 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 10 3.5.5. Key messages • Vaccination is a key tool for prevention, control, and eradication of animal diseases. It also serves as an important risk mitigation measure alongside surveillance, quarantine, and biosecurity, especially for diseases like African swine fever or avian influenza, which urgently require vaccine solutions. • The WOAH Terrestrial Manual and Code set comprehensive standards for vaccine approval, control and registration (including aspects of quality, efficacy, and safety). They include general vaccination chapters and disease-specific provisions. In some cases, the use of vaccination does not affect the status of a free country or zone if surveillance supports the absence of infection. These standards result from rigorous scientific risk assessments by WOAH expert groups and are informed by Member experiences. Their consistent implementation is crucial. • WOAH Members must not only adopt but actively apply WOAH standards in international trade (both imports and exports), as these standards underpin safe and science-based trade practices. Standards are too often applied inconsistently depending on a country’s disease situation. This selective application should be addressed. • World Trade Organization (WTO) dispute panels have consistently ruled in favour of countries that apply WOAH standards. To avoid disputes, greater trust and reliance on these standards is needed among trading partners. • International standards, like those from WOAH, are essential for safe trade. The WTO’s Sanitary and Phytosanitary (SPS) Agreement requires health measures to be science-based and encourages harmonisation with WOAH guidelines, including those on vaccination. • Since 1995, about 33% of SPS Committee trade concerns have related to animal health. In the past decade, up to 80% have questioned whether national requirements align with WOAH standards, especially in relation to vaccination and zoning. WOAH guidance has been central to resolving major disputes on avian influenza, FMD, and ASF. • Effective implementation of WOAH standards requires coordination between Veterinary Services, health and trade ministries, and public and private stakeholders. Developing countries, in particular, need support to engage in standards setting and implementation. The WOAH Observatory and SPS Committee play key roles in monitoring and supporting this process. • For veterinary students, vaccination is central to disease prevention. ‘Prevention is better than a cure’ is a principle which is carried forward into professional veterinary life. • While veterinary education equips students to understand vaccination science, they are less prepared for the challenges of public communication and the economic dimensions of vaccine use. Social media misinformation spreads fast, and thoughtful, well-prepared responses are needed. • There is a need for clearer guidance and dialogue with experienced professionals to better prepare students for real-world implementation challenges around vaccination. • Vaccination is not merely as a disease prevention tool, but a core pillar of resilient and sustainable animal health systems – critical for protecting animal and public health, supporting livelihoods and enabling prudent antimicrobial use. • Countries are urged to consistently apply WOAH’s international standards, ensuring that the fear of trade restrictions does not deter vaccination, and that Veterinary Services are equipped to implement these standards effectively. • WOAH emphasises the importance of transparency through WAHIS reporting, sharing best practices, and leveraging its scientific network to foster trust and support the safe trade of vaccinated animals and their products. 92GS – PARIS, MAY 2025 11 3.6. Session 6: Final discussion, wrap up and conclusions 3.6.1. Background The final session of the 2025 Animal Health Forum provided an opportunity to consolidate insights across all technical and high-level discussions. Rapporteurs summarised cross-cutting themes and highlighted the most significant technical, political, and socioeconomic takeaways from the Forum. Selected questions submitted by participants during the event were used to prompt discussion, with the floor also opened for live interventions and feedback. This enabled a broad, collective reflection on how to move from policy to implementation. This session concluded with the nomination of a drafting team tasked with developing the Animal Health Forum’s resolution. 3.6.2. Objectives • Summarise key insights and discussions from Animal Health Forum • Nominate a drafting team to develop the Animal Health Forum resolution 3.6.3. Moderator Professor Christine Middlemiss, Chief Veterinary Officer and WOAH Delegate of the United Kingdom, WOAH Council Member 3.6.4. Panellists Dr Montserrat Arroyo (WOAH Deputy Director General for Standards Setting and Implementation); Dr Baptiste Dungu (Technical Item Rapporteur), Dr Meritxell Donadeu (Technical Item Rapporteur) 4. Conclusion The Animal Health Forum on Veterinary Vaccines and Vaccination, held during the 92nd WOAH General Session, was welcomed by Delegates and partners as a valuable space to address barriers to effective vaccine use. Discussions reinforced that vaccines are essential to disease control, antimicrobial resistance reduction, and sustainable food systems, but remain underused due to challenges in availability, access, and uptake. The Forum highlighted the need for action across the vaccine value chain – including development, regulatory harmonisation, strategic deployment, and public communication – to enable timely, effective, and trusted vaccination programmes that support disease control, safe trade, and sustainable animal health systems. The Forum reaffirmed WOAH’s leadership role in guiding Members and partners from policy to practical action. While solutions must be context-specific, shared goals around equity, effectiveness, and sustainability were clear. Resolution 29, based on the Technical Item and Forum outcomes, outlines the agreed recommendations to strengthen veterinary vaccination worldwide. The resolution is available here.

The aim is to provide a snapshot of some of the most exciting work published in the various research areas of the journal.

Abstract
--------

Zoonotic diseases represent a heavy global burden, causing important economic losses, impacting animal health and production, and costing millions of human lives. The vaccination of animals and humans to prevent inter-species zoonotic disease transmission is an important intervention. However, efforts to develop and implement vaccine interventions to reduce zoonotic disease impacts are often limited to the veterinary and agricultural sectors and do not reflect the shared burden of disease. Multisectoral collaboration, including co-development opportunities for human and animal vaccines, expanding vaccine use to include animal reservoirs such as wildlife, and strategically using vaccines to interrupt complex transmission cycles is needed. Addressing zoonoses requires a multi-faceted One Health approach, wherein vaccinating people and animals plays a critical role. 

Keywords: 

[vaccine](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=vaccine); [zoonotic](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=zoonotic); [animal](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=animal); [emerging](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=emerging); [One Health](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=One+Health)

1. Introduction
---------------

Scientists estimate that 60% of diseases in humans can be attributed to origins in and spreading from animals (i.e., zoonoses), and many of these diseases have high mortality rates and the potential to cause epidemics and pandemics [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/10/7/993#B1-vaccines-10-00993)]. Zoonotic diseases are responsible for approximately 2.7 million deaths and 2.5 billion human illnesses annually, in addition to impacting livestock production and food security [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/10/7/993#B2-vaccines-10-00993)]. SARS-CoV-2, which has zoonotic origins and emerged as a global pandemic in 2020, had caused over 4.4 M human deaths globally by mid-2021 alone [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/10/7/993#B3-vaccines-10-00993)]. In addition to human and animal health impacts, zoonotic disease burdens include substantial economic damage due to the cost of human illness, lost livestock production, and disrupted ecosystem health [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/10/7/993#B4-vaccines-10-00993),[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/10/7/993#B5-vaccines-10-00993)]. The potential for zoonotic diseases to affect human, animal, plant, and environmental health, global food security, and economic stability highlights the need for effective interventions that target prevention at multiple levels.

Vaccines are one of the most important public health achievements of the 20th century. From Edward Jenner using cowpox (Variolae vaccinae) to protect against smallpox in the 18th century, Pasteur’s discovery of how to inactivate the rabies virus to save human lives through vaccination, to the need to rapidly create effective vaccines during the explosive SARS-CoV-2 pandemic, vaccination is undeniably one of the most effective tools to prevent, control, and even eradicate disease [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/10/7/993#B6-vaccines-10-00993),[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/10/7/993#B7-vaccines-10-00993)]. enriched_points.expanded_content: Navigating complex regulatory frameworks for vaccine approval often delays market entry, impacting vaccine availability and requiring significant resources, as highlighted in recent industry discussions.
The development and production of novel vaccines incur high costs, which can restrict accessibility for farmers, particularly in low-income regions, thereby limiting overall vaccination uptake.
Public skepticism towards vaccines can arise from misinformation and distrust, making effective communication essential to gain community support and enhance vaccination acceptance.
Challenges such as inadequate infrastructure for vaccine distribution, storage, and administration significantly hinder timely access to vaccines in rural and underserved areas.
